ontology.foodon_gap_note: No exact FoodOn URI exists for Soan Papdi. Mapped to the nearest parent class 'confection food product' as a defensible approximate anchor. A species-level FoodOn entry for Soan Papdi does not exist in the current release.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 does not contain a specific entry for Soan Papdi. Mapped to 'Sugar confectionery' (A0CKP) as the closest defensible FoodEx2 category given the high sugar content and confectionery nature of the product.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT code identified for Soan Papdi or its parent confectionery category in the clinical terminology. SNOMED CT is not applicable for this traditional Indian sweet.

preparation_method: Roasting gram flour and all-purpose flour in ghee
Cooking sugar syrup to hard-ball stage
Combining roasted flour with hot sugar syrup
Manual pulling and stretching of the mixture to form fine fibrous strands
Garnishing with chopped pistachios and almonds

serving.natural_language_portion: A typical serving of Soan Papdi is approximately 35 grams, equivalent to two to three small cubes, as commonly portioned for festival gifting or as a light sweet snack.

nutrient_source.title: Nutritive Value of Indian Foods
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Soan Papdi is a traditional Indian confection. Nutrient values are adapted from ICMR-NIN 'Nutritive Value of Indian Foods' for gram flour-based Indian sweets made with ghee and sugar. Specific Soan Papdi values are not individually listed in the NIN tables; values represent a best-fit adaptation from besan-ghee-sugar confection data. Saturated fat estimated from ghee content (~24.5g total fat, ghee being predominantly saturated). Monounsaturated and polyunsaturated fat set to -1 as detailed fatty acid breakdown is not available in NIN for this preparation. Cholesterol set to -1 as not reported. Water content set to -1 as not reported for this confection.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2021
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / American Journal of Clinical Nutrition
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement for Soan Papdi exists in the University of Sydney GI Database. GI of 72 is estimated by analogy to refined sugar-maida confections with high free sugar content (comparable to plain biscuits and sugar-based Indian sweets with GI 70–75 per Atkinson et al., 2021, AJCN). Glycemic load per serving calculated as: (GI × carbohydrate_g per serving) / 100 = (72 × 21.7) / 100 ≈ 15.6. Carbohydrate per 35g serving = 62.0 × 0.35 = 21.7g.

hypertension.key_factor: High caloric density and saturated fat from ghee; low sodium content
hypertension.guidance: Soan Papdi is low in sodium (20 mg/100g) and does not directly raise blood pressure through salt load, but its high saturated fat content (~15g/100g from ghee) and caloric density (492 kcal/100g) may contribute to weight gain and arterial stiffness; individuals with hypertension should limit intake to occasional small portions of 15–20g.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Very high free sugar content (52g/100g) and high glycemic index (GI 72) causing rapid postprandial blood glucose elevation
type2_diabetes.guidance: Soan Papdi should be avoided or strictly limited by individuals with Type 2 Diabetes, as its very high free sugar content (52g/100g) and high GI of 72 cause rapid and significant postprandial blood glucose spikes that are difficult to manage with standard insulin or oral hypoglycaemic regimens.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: High saturated fat from ghee and high sugar content contributing to dyslipidaemia and atherogenic risk
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Individuals with cardiovascular disease should minimise consumption of Soan Papdi due to its high saturated fat content (~15g/100g from ghee) and high free sugar (52g/100g), both of which are associated with elevated LDL cholesterol, triglycerides, and increased atherogenic risk per current AHA dietary guidelines.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: High refined sugar and refined flour content with pro-inflammatory potential
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Individuals with rheumatoid arthritis should limit Soan Papdi as its high refined sugar (52g/100g) and refined maida content may promote systemic inflammation and exacerbate joint symptoms in sensitive individuals.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Soan Papdi is a traditional North Indian confection celebrated for its uniquely flaky, fibrous, and melt-in-the-mouth texture. It is prepared by roasting a blend of gram flour (besan) and all-purpose flour (maida) in clarified butter (ghee), then combining the roasted flour with a hot, concentrated sugar syrup cooked to the hard-ball stage. The defining technique involves manually pulling and stretching the warm mixture repeatedly until it transforms into delicate, hair-like strands. These strands are layered, compressed into rectangular blocks or cubes, and garnished with finely chopped pistachios and almonds, with cardamom added for its characteristic aromatic warmth. The result is a light, airy sweet with a crisp yet instantly dissolving texture and a refined, not-overpowering sweetness. Nutritionally, Soan Papdi is energy-dense at approximately 492 kcal per 100g, with 62g of carbohydrates (of which 52g is free sugar), 24.5g of fat (predominantly saturated fat from ghee), and 5.8g of protein from the gram flour. Its glycemic index is estimated at 72 (high), making it unsuitable for individuals managing Type 2 Diabetes or cardiovascular disease. It is a staple festive gift during Diwali, Raksha Bandhan, and other major Indian celebrations, and is widely available across India and in Indian diaspora communities globally. A standard serving is approximately 35g (two to three cubes), providing around 172 kcal and 21.7g of carbohydrates.

meal_context: festive sweet during Diwali and Raksha Bandhan
gift-giving during Indian festivals and holidays

confidence_reason: Nutrient values are adapted from ICMR-NIN data for gram flour-based Indian sweets made with ghee and sugar, as Soan Papdi is not individually listed in the NIN tables. The adaptation is defensible given the known ingredient composition. GI is an analogous estimate from University of Sydney GI Database comparable confections. Saturated fat is estimated from ghee content. Detailed fatty acid profile, cholesterol, and water content are not available from the approved source for this specific preparation.
notes: 1. Nutrient values adapted from ICMR-NIN 'Nutritive Value of Indian Foods' for besan-ghee-sugar confections; Soan Papdi is not individually tabulated in the NIN database. 2. Saturated fat (15.0g/100g) is estimated based on ghee being the primary fat source (ghee is approximately 60–65% saturated fat; 24.5g total fat × 0.61 ≈ 15.0g). 3. monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, trans_fat_g, cholesterol_mg, and water_g are set to -1: detailed fatty acid breakdown and moisture data for Soan Papdi are not available in ICMR-NIN or USDA FoodData Central for this traditional pulled-sugar confection preparation. 4. vitamin_c_mg is 0.0: Soan Papdi contains no vitamin C-bearing ingredients; this is a confirmed zero consistent with the ingredient profile (no fruits, no vegetables). 5. GI of 72 is an analogous estimate based on refined sugar-maida confections from the University of Sydney GI Database / Atkinson et al. 2021 AJCN international GI tables; no direct GI measurement for Soan Papdi exists in the approved database. 6. Glycemic load per serving = (72 × 21.7) / 100 = 15.6 (serving size 35g, carbohydrate per serving = 62.0 × 0.35 = 21.7g). 7. Portions in the input JSON contained four identical duplicate entries; deduplicated to a single standard serving of 35g. 8. This is an Indian dish; multilingual naming requirements apply for Hindi and Tamil as relevant diaspora languages; Chinese, Malay, Japanese, Korean, Indonesian, Thai, and Vietnamese local names are not applicable for this North Indian confection.
